recipes.name: Künefe
steps: Make Syrup: Boil sugar and water with lemon juice for 15 minutes. Add rosewater. Cool.
Shred kataifi dough into smaller pieces using your hands.
Pour melted butter over the dough and mix well.
Press half the dough into a baking dish, packing firmly.
Spread cheese evenly over the dough.
Top with the remaining dough, pressing down gently.
Bake for 30-40 minutes until golden and crisp.
Invert onto a serving plate or pour syrup directly over the top.
making_tips: The kataifi should be shredded as fine as possible for the best texture.
Use a cheese that melts well but holds its shape.
The syrup should be cold when poured over the hot künefe.
Serve immediately to maintain the crisp pastry.
Künefe is best eaten fresh from the oven.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Commercial künefe is often soggy. Homemade has the perfect crisp pastry and stringy cheese.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Cheese provides calcium and protein. Pistachios offer healthy fats.

recipes.name: Şekerpare
steps: Make Syrup: Boil sugar and water with lemon juice for 15 minutes. Cool.
In a bowl, cream butter and sugar until fluffy.
Mix flour, semolina, and baking powder. Add to the butter mixture.
Roll into small balls and flatten slightly.
Press an almond into the center of each.
Pour cold syrup over the hot cookies.
making_tips: Semolina gives şekerpare its signature texture.
The syrup must be cold when poured over the hot cookies.
Don't overbake—they should be golden, not dark.
The almond in the center is traditional.
These cookies improve after resting in the syrup.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Commercial şekerpare is often dry. Homemade is syrup-soaked and tender.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Almonds provide vitamin E and healthy fats. Semolina offers B vitamins.

recipes.name: Sütlaç (Turkish Rice Pudding)
steps: Rinse rice and soak in water for 30 minutes.
Drain rice and place in a pot with 500ml water.
Cook until rice is soft and water is absorbed.
Add milk and sugar, stirring constantly.
Dissolve cornstarch in a little water and add to the pot.
making_tips: The rice should be very soft before adding milk.
Stir constantly to prevent sticking.
The pudding thickens as it cools.
Serve in small clay bowls for the traditional presentation.
A favorite across Turkey and the Middle East.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Commercial rice pudding is often watery. Homemade is thick, creamy, and comforting.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Milk provides calcium. Rice offers carbohydrates. Cinnamon aids blood sugar regulation.

recipes.name: Boza (Turkish Fermented Millet Drink)
steps: Mix millet flour with water to form a thin batter.
Cook over medium heat, stirring constantly, until thickened (about 30 minutes).
Cover and let ferment for 24-48 hours at room temperature.
The boza should be slightly tangy and fizzy.
Top with roasted chickpeas and cinnamon.
making_tips: Boza is a traditional Turkish winter drink.
Fermentation time varies depending on temperature.
The drink should be tangy but not sour.
Roasted chickpeas and cinnamon are essential toppings.
It is served in brass cups in Istanbul.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Bottled boza has preservatives. Homemade is effervescent and authentic.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Millet provides B vitamins and fiber. The drink contains probiotics.

recipes.name: Gaz (Iranian Nougat)

Main: 200g sap of Persian manna plant (or substitute with gum arabic)

steps: In a pot, combine sugar, water, and glucose syrup. Heat to 120°C.
In a stand mixer, beat egg whites until stiff.
Slowly pour the hot syrup into the egg whites while beating.
Continue beating until thick and glossy.
Add the manna sap (or gum arabic dissolved in a little water).
Pour into a lined pan and press down firmly.
making_tips: Gaz is Iran's most famous nougat.
The manna sap gives it a unique texture.
A candy thermometer is essential.
The pistachios should be high quality.
Traditionally served with Persian tea.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Commercial gaz often uses substitutes. Homemade has authentic ingredients.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Pistachios provide healthy fats and protein. Egg whites offer protein.

recipes.name: Faloodeh (Persian Rosewater Ice)
steps: Make Syrup: Boil sugar and water until dissolved. Add rosewater and lime juice. Cool.
Cook rice noodles according to package instructions. Cool and cut into small pieces.
Combine the cooled syrup and noodles.
Stir every 30 minutes for 2-3 hours to prevent ice crystals.
The mixture should be slushy and semi-frozen.
Top with sour cherry syrup and pistachios.
making_tips: Faloodeh is Iran's signature summer dessert.
The stirring is essential for the right texture.
Rosewater is the key flavor.
Sour cherry syrup is the traditional topping.
This is a refreshing Persian dessert.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Commercial faloodeh often lacks real rosewater. Homemade has authentic flavor.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Pistachios provide healthy fats. Sour cherry syrup offers trace vitamins.

recipes.name: Sholeh Zard (Persian Saffron Pudding)
steps: Rinse rice and soak in water for 30 minutes.
In a large pot, cook rice in water until very soft (about 30 minutes).
Add milk, sugar, and saffron (ground and dissolved in a little hot water).
Cook over low heat, stirring frequently, until thickened.
Decorate with slivered almonds in a pattern.
making_tips: Sholeh zard is a celebratory Persian dessert.
The rice must be very soft and broken down.
Saffron gives the golden color and distinct flavor.
The decoration is as important as the taste.
Often served at weddings and celebrations.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: This traditional Persian dessert is best homemade.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Milk provides calcium. Saffron offers antioxidants. Almonds add vitamin E.

recipes.name: Sekanjebin (Persian Mint & Vinegar Drink)
steps: Make Syrup: Boil sugar and water until dissolved.
Add vinegar and mint. Simmer for 5 minutes.
To serve, mix 1 part syrup with 3 parts cold water.
Garnish with fresh mint and cucumber slices.
making_tips: Sekanjebin is a refreshing Persian drink.
The vinegar gives it a sweet-tangy flavor.
Cucumbers add a cooling element.
Traditionally served in summer.
This is a taste of ancient Persia.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: This simple drink is best made fresh.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Mint aids digestion. Vinegar has probiotic properties.

recipes.name: Ma'amoul (Shortbread Cookies)
steps: Make Dough: Mix flour, semolina, and butter until crumbly.
Add sugar, orange blossom water, and milk.
Knead into a soft dough. Rest for 30 minutes.
Prepare fillings: dates are ready to use; nuts should be mixed with a little sugar.
Take small pieces of dough, flatten, and fill.
Seal and place in a ma'amoul mold.
Bake at 180°C for 15-18 minutes until lightly golden.
making_tips: The wooden ma'amoul mold is essential for traditional patterns.
The dough should be worked quickly to keep it from drying.
Date and nut fillings are both traditional.
These are holiday cookies for Eid and Easter.
They improve after a day.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Commercial ma'amoul is often dry. Homemade is melt-in-your-mouth.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Dates provide iron and fiber. Pistachios offer healthy fats.

recipes.name: Basbousa (Semolina Cake)
steps: Make Syrup: Boil sugar and water for 15 minutes. Add orange blossom water. Cool.
Mix semolina, sugar, coconut, and baking powder.
Add melted butter, yogurt, and vanilla. Mix well.
Spread in a greased baking dish.
Press an almond into the center of each piece.
Pour cold syrup over the hot cake.
Cut along the score marks and serve.
making_tips: The almond on each piece is traditional.
Basbousa is beloved across the Middle East.
The cake should be moist and syrup-soaked.
Orange blossom water is essential for the flavor.
Cut before baking for neat pieces.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Commercial basbousa is often dry. Homemade is moist and fragrant.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Coconut provides healthy fats. Semolina offers B vitamins.

recipes.name: Luqaimat (Fried Dough Balls)
steps: Mix flour, yeast, sugar, salt, and milk powder.
Add warm water gradually to form a soft dough.
Cover and let rise for 1 hour.
Using a wet spoon, drop small balls of dough into hot oil.
Fry until golden and puffed, turning once.
Drizzle with date syrup or honey.
making_tips: Luqaimat is a Saudi favorite during Ramadan.
The dough should be sticky and soft.
Date syrup is the traditional accompaniment.
They should be crisp outside and fluffy inside.
Best served fresh and warm.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Fresh luqaimat with date syrup is a Saudi specialty.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Date syrup provides trace minerals. The dish is a festive treat.

recipes.name: Saudi Coffee (Qahwa)
steps: Place ground coffee and spices in a dallah (traditional pot).
Bring to a boil over medium heat.
Reduce heat and simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Serve with sweet dates on the side.
making_tips: Saudi coffee is light roasted and spiced.
The coffee is usually unsweetened—dates provide the sweetness.
Served in small cups without handles.
The dallah pot is the traditional vessel.
Coffee is a sign of hospitality in the Gulf.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: The ritual of Saudi coffee is best experienced at home.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Coffee provides antioxidants. Cardamom aids digestion.

recipes.name: Qatayef (Stuffed Pancakes)

Filling: 100g walnuts or pistachios, ground with sugar

steps: Make Batter: Mix flour, semolina, yeast, sugar, and salt. Add water gradually.
Make Syrup: Boil sugar and water for 15 minutes. Add orange blossom water. Cool.
Heat a non-stick pan over medium heat.
Pour small rounds of batter—cook until bubbles appear.
Place filling on one half of each pancake.
Fold over and press edges to seal.
Heat oil and fry the qatayef until golden.
making_tips: Qatayef is the ultimate Ramadan dessert in Egypt.
The pancakes should be cooked on one side only.
The cheese filling is traditional and gooey.
Fried qatayef is preferred; baked is also an option.
Syrup should be cold when the qatayef is hot.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Fresh qatayef is a Ramadan tradition—best made at home.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Cheese provides calcium and protein. Nuts offer healthy fats.

recipes.name: Karkadeh (Hibiscus Tea)
steps: In a pot, combine petals and water.
Bring to a boil, then reduce heat.
Remove from heat and steep for 30 minutes.
Add sugar and stir to dissolve.
making_tips: Karkadeh can be served hot or cold.
The cold version is the most popular in Egypt.
The tea should be deep ruby red.
Adjust sweetness to taste.
A refreshing and healthy Egyptian drink.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Bottled karkadeh has preservatives. Homemade is pure and vibrant.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Hibiscus is rich in antioxidants. Helps lower blood pressure.

recipes.name: Knafeh (Jordanian Nabulsi)

Syrup: 1 tbsp rosewater or orange blossom water

steps: Make Syrup: Boil sugar and water for 15 minutes. Add rosewater. Cool.
Pour melted butter over the dough and mix.
Press half the dough into a pan.
Spread cheese evenly over the dough.
Pour cold syrup over hot knafeh.
making_tips: Nabulsi cheese is the authentic choice for Jordanian knafeh.
The contrast between hot pastry and cold syrup is key.
Knafeh is Jordan's pride.
Serve immediately for the best texture.
Traditionally eaten by hand.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Authentic Nabulsi knafeh is a Jordanian specialty best fresh.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Cheese provides calcium and protein. Pistachios offer healthy fats.

recipes.name: Namoura (Semolina Cake)
steps: Make Syrup: Boil sugar and water for 15 minutes. Add orange blossom water. Cool.
Mix semolina, sugar, and baking powder.
Add yogurt, melted butter, vanilla, and orange blossom water.
Press an almond into each piece.
Pour cold syrup over hot cake.
making_tips: Namoura is similar to basbousa but uses yogurt.
Orange blossom water gives the signature floral note.
The almond decoration is essential.
Syrup absorption makes it moist.
A Middle Eastern tea-time favorite.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Fresh namoura is far superior to commercial versions.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Semolina provides B vitamins. Almonds offer healthy fats.

recipes.name: Limonana (Mint Lemonade)
steps: In a blender, combine lemon juice, sugar, and mint with a little water.
Blend until mint is finely chopped.
making_tips: Limonana is a Jordanian invention.
The mint should be completely blended.
This is the ultimate Middle Eastern summer cooler.
Serve immediately.
Adjust sugar and mint to taste.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Freshly blended limonana is a Jordanian specialty.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Lemons provide vitamin C. Mint aids digestion.

recipes.name: Mhalabiyeh (Milk Custard)
steps: In a pot, heat remaining milk with sugar and cardamom.
Cook over medium heat, stirring constantly.
making_tips: Mhalabiyeh is Lebanon's silky milk pudding.
The texture should be smooth and creamy.
Rosewater is the classic flavor.
Serve in beautiful glasses.
The topping adds texture.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Homemade mhalabiyeh is silky and perfectly flavored.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Milk provides calcium. Pistachios offer healthy fats.

recipes.name: Sfouf (Turmeric Semolina Cake)
steps: Mix semolina, flour, sugar, turmeric, and baking powder.
Add oil and milk. Mix to a smooth batter.
making_tips: Turmeric gives sfouf its golden color.
It's not sweet but subtly flavored.
Pine nuts are the traditional topping.
This is a Lebanese tea-time favorite.
The texture should be dense and moist.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Fresh sfouf with its golden color is a Lebanese specialty.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Turmeric has anti-inflammatory properties. Pine nuts offer healthy fats.

recipes.name: Malabi (Rosewater Milk Custard)
steps: In a pot, heat remaining milk with sugar.
Cook over medium heat, stirring constantly.
making_tips: Malabi is Israel's favorite milk dessert.
The texture is silky and light.
Rosewater gives the signature floral note.
The toppings add color and texture.
Often served at street food markets.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Fresh malabi with proper rosewater is best.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Milk provides calcium. Pistachios offer healthy fats.

recipes.name: Sufganiyot (Jelly Doughnuts)
steps: Mix flour, sugar, yeast, and salt.
Add water, egg yolks, and butter. Knead until smooth.
Punch down dough. Roll to 1cm thickness.
Cut into rounds and let rise 30 minutes.
Fry doughnuts until golden on both sides.
Fill with jam using a piping bag.
making_tips: Sufganiyot are Israel's Hanukkah treat.
The dough should be light and fluffy.
Fill with jam just before serving.
Powdered sugar is essential.
They should be eaten fresh.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Fresh sufganiyot are a Hanukkah necessity.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Eggs provide protein. The treat is a celebration food.

recipes.name: Balaleet (Sweet Vermicelli)
steps: Boil vermicelli in water until cooked. Drain.
In a pan, melt ghee and add noodles.
Make Egg Crepe: Beat eggs with salt.
Fry a thin crepe in a pan.
making_tips: Balaleet is a Qatari breakfast favorite.
The saffron gives it a golden color.
The egg crepe is the traditional topping.
Sweet and savory in one dish.
A unique Gulf specialty.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: This traditional Gulf dish is best homemade.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Eggs provide protein. Saffron offers antioxidants.

recipes.name: Esh Asaraya (Bread Pudding)
steps: Tear bread into pieces and place in a dish.
In a pot, heat milk with condensed milk and sugar.
Pour the milk mixture over the bread.
making_tips: Esh asaraya is a popular Qatari dessert.
The bread absorbs the milky liquid.
It is often served during celebrations.
Rosewater gives a floral note.
A comforting dessert.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Traditional Qatari bread pudding is best fresh.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Milk provides calcium. Pistachios offer healthy fats.

recipes.name: Emirati Coffee (Gahwa)
steps: Place ground coffee and spices in a dallah pot.
Reduce heat and simmer for 10-15 minutes.
making_tips: Emirati coffee is light and flavored with cardamom.
The dallah pot is the traditional vessel.
Coffee is served unsweetened with dates.
A symbol of hospitality in the UAE.
Often served in small cups.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: The ritual of Emirati coffee is best at home.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Coffee provides antioxidants. Dates offer iron and fiber.

recipes.name: Bint Al-Sahn (Yemeni Honey Cake)
steps: Make Dough: Mix flour, yeast, salt, and water. Knead until smooth.
Brush each layer with melted ghee.
Stack the layers on top of each other.
Place the stack in a greased pan.
Bake at 180°C for 20-25 minutes until golden.
making_tips: Bint Al-Sahn is Yemen's national dessert.
The layers should be paper-thin.
Ghee is essential for the authentic flavor.
Yemeni honey is famous worldwide.
Best eaten by hand.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: This traditional Yemeni cake is best homemade.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Honey provides trace minerals. Ghee offers vitamin A.

recipes.name: Qishr (Yemeni Coffee-Husk Tea)

Main: 50g dried coffee husks (or coffee cherry tea)

steps: In a pot, combine husks, water, and ginger.
Reduce heat and simmer for 15 minutes.
Add sugar and stir to dissolve.
making_tips: Qishr is a Yemeni traditional drink.
It has a mild coffee-like flavor.
Ginger gives it a warming spice.
It is frugal as it uses coffee byproducts.
Served at social gatherings.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: This is a traditional Yemeni drink, best made fresh.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Ginger aids digestion. Coffee husks provide trace antioxidants.

recipes.name: Knafeh Nabulsiyeh (Palestinian)
steps: Make Syrup: Boil sugar and water for 15 minutes. Add orange blossom water. Cool.
Pour melted butter over the dough and mix.
Press half the dough into a pan.
Pour cold syrup over the hot knafeh.
making_tips: Nabulsi cheese is the authentic Palestinian choice.
Knafeh Nabulsiyeh is Palestine's pride.
The syrup should be cold; the knafeh hot.
Orange blossom water is essential.
Serve immediately.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Authentic Nabulsi knafeh is a Palestinian treasure.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Cheese provides calcium and protein. Pistachios offer healthy fats.

recipes.name: Barazek (Syrian Sesame Cookies)
steps: Cream butter and sugar until fluffy.
Add egg, orange blossom water, and yeast.
Gradually add flour to form a dough.
Roll small pieces of dough into balls.
Dip one side in sesame seeds, the other in pistachios.
making_tips: Barazek is a famous Syrian cookie.
The sesame and pistachio coating is distinctive.
The cookie should be crisp and crumbly.
Often served with Syrian coffee.
A beautiful and delicious cookie.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Syrian barazek is a specialty—fresh is best.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Sesame seeds provide calcium and iron. Pistachios offer healthy fats.

recipes.name: Syrian Baklava
steps: Make Syrup: Boil sugar, water, and lemon juice for 15 minutes. Add orange blossom water. Cool.
Layer 10 phyllo sheets, brushing each with butter.
Pour cold syrup over hot baklava.
making_tips: Syrian baklava uses pistachios and is lighter.
Clarified butter (semn) is traditional.
The syrup is less sweet than other versions.
It should be crisp and not too syrupy.
A Damascus specialty.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Authentic Syrian baklava is a Damascus specialty.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Pistachios provide healthy fats and protein. Butter offers vitamin A.

recipes.name: Syrian Tea
steps: In a teapot, add tea leaves.
Pour boiling water over the tea.
Add cinnamon or cardamom if desired.
making_tips: Syrian tea is strong and heavily sweetened.
Spices like cinnamon and cardamom are common.
Tea is a sign of hospitality in Syria.
Often served with pistachio treats.
The tea should be dark amber.
recipes.better_than_store_bought: Syrian tea with spices is best made at home.
recipes.hidden_nutrients: Tea provides antioxidants. Cinnamon aids blood sugar regulation.
